questions.question: What is the difference between an SLO and an SLA?
options: An SLO is the internal target the team commits to; an SLA is a customer-facing contract with penalties, usually set looser than the SLO
An SLA is stricter than an SLO
An SLO only applies to latency
questions.explanation: The SLO is the goal you hold yourselves to internally (e.g. 99.9%). The SLA is the promise to customers with consequences if missed (e.g. 99.5% or a refund). You set the SLO stricter than the SLA to have a safety margin before breaking a contract. The SLI is the actual measured number.

questions.question: Why is 'aim for 100% reliability' the wrong goal?
options: It's impossibly expensive and unnecessary — some failure is acceptable, which is exactly what the error budget formalizes
questions.explanation: Perfect reliability costs more than all the prior nines combined and users can't perceive the difference for most products. The error budget exists because some failure is acceptable; chasing zero wastes enormous effort for imperceptible gain.

questions.question: Why should a latency SLO use a percentile (like p99) rather than the average?
options: The average hides the slow tail — a few very slow requests barely move the average but represent real users suffering; percentiles capture the tail
Averages only work for errors, not latency
questions.explanation: If 99 requests take 10ms and one takes 5s, the average looks fine (~60ms) but a real user waited 5s. Percentiles (p95, p99, p99.9) expose the tail that users actually complain about — and in fan-out requests one slow dependency at p99 can dominate the whole request.

questions.question: An SLO of 99.9% over 100M monthly requests gives what error budget, and how is it used?
options: 100,000 allowed failures/month — a 'currency': ship features while budget remains, freeze risky changes when it's exhausted
questions.explanation: 0.1% of 100M = 100,000 allowed failures per month. The error budget reframes reliability as a currency that resolves the feature-vs-stability fight: budget remaining → take risks and ship; budget exhausted → freeze and fix until back in budget.

questions.question: Why do you provision capacity for peak load with headroom (e.g. 70% utilization) rather than to exactly fit the load?
options: Near 100% utilization latency explodes (queueing) and there's no slack to absorb traffic spikes or a server failure
Because peak load never actually happens
questions.explanation: A system near saturation has rapidly growing queue/wait times, so latency spikes and breaks the SLO. Running at 100% also leaves no room to absorb a burst or redistribute a failed server's load. Headroom (e.g. 70% target) provisions for peak plus failures plus growth.
